retrieved_docs.title: Drug and Alcohol Use (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol: As many as 70% of individuals relapse after psychosocial treatment alone Naltrexone in toxicity and Supportive care. Naltrexone can be started while the patient is still drinking. Disulfiram should be used by abstinent patients to maintain abstinence, while acamprosate should be used once abstinence is achieved. Disulfiram should only be used in patients who are highly motivated to maintain abstinence. Treat Wernicke-Korsakoff with vitamin B-1 (thiamine) intravenously.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, it is paramount to diagnose and address underlying AUD, which often involves starting a medication and ensuring appropriate outpatient follow-up. The 3 FDA-approved medications for AUD include naltrexone, acamprosate, and disulfiram. These can often be safely started before discharge and reduce 30-day readmission. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Disulfiram -- Indications -- Food and Drug Administration-Approved Indication
retrieved_docs.content: It is a second-line option (acamprosate and naltrexone are first-line treatments) in patients with sufficient physician supervision. Disulfiram is safe and efficient in supervised short-term and long-term treatment of individuals dependent on alcohol but who are motivated to discontinue alcohol use. [1] The American Psychiatric Association (APA) 2018 guidelines endorse the use of disulfiram for alcohol use disorder. According to APA recommendations, disulfiram should be considered for patients exhibiting moderate to severe alcohol use disorder under specific conditions. This includes individuals with a primary goal of achieving abstinence and those who express a preference for disulfiram or demonstrate intolerance or lack of response to naltrexone and acamprosate. Furthermore, candidates for disulfiram treatment should possess the capability to comprehend the risks associated with alcohol consumption while taking the medication, and they should have no contraindications to its use. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Addiction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologically, alcohol dependence is treatable with disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. [33] Each has its place in alcohol dependence and addiction. Disulfiram is effective in managing a patient who recently quit and needs help maintaining abstinence, as any consumed alcohol causes relatively quick hangover-type symptoms to deter further drinking. Naltrexone removes the sensation of reward or pleasure with drinking, and acamprosate minimizes the initial withdrawal symptoms. Alcohol intoxication during episodes of relapse is treatable with long-acting benzodiazepines such as chlordiazepoxide or diazepam. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Addiction Relapse Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Disulfiram is a medication that inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase resulting in the build-up of acetaldehyde, which produces uncomfortable physical effects. As a result, disulfiram acts as a deterrent against an alcohol relapse until the body metabolizes the medications. One significant challenge regarding the use of disulfiram is non-adherence. Supervised treatment with disulfiram has correlated with an increased time to relapse and a reduced number of drinking days. [13] Disulfiram has been shown superior to naltrexone and acamprosate but only when used in observed dosing. Such results are unlikely outside of observed therapy due to frequent discontinuation.

retrieved_docs.title: Veteran and Military Mental Health Issues -- Treatment / Management -- Substance Use Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacotherapy: Several pharmacotherapies have garnered support in managing SUDs, primarily by reducing cravings or withdrawal symptoms to incentivize abstinence or reduce barriers to quitting. [17] The 3 medications approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for AUDs include naltrexone, acamprosate, and disulfiram. The FDA approves methadone, buprenorphine, and naltrexone for opioid use disorders (OUDs). However, there are currently no off-label drugs to treat cocaine or marijuana use disorders. [84]

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol Use Disorder: Screening, Evaluation, and Management -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The goal of AUD treatment is to reduce alcohol intake or have complete cessation; this includes pharmacotherapy with or without behavioral treatments. Three US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-cleared medications for AUD are discussed below:

retrieved_docs.title: Substance Use Disorder (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA approves acamprosate, naltrexone, and disulfiram to treat alcohol use disorder. [28] Disulfiram, an aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor, produces an unpleasant set of symptoms such as headache, nausea, vomiting, flushing, dizziness, and weakness when alcohol is consumed to prevent further alcohol consumption. [29] Naltrexone blocks the central mu-receptor, reducing cravings, and aids in preventing relapse to alcohol use. [30] Acamprosate aids in reducing withdrawal symptoms by countering excessive NMDA receptor activation from alcohol withdrawal. In the clinical setting, it is important to include vitamin B1 and vitamin B9, along with multivitamins supplements, to address any nutritional deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol Use Disorder: Screening, Evaluation, and Management -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Acamprosate : Acamprosate is another effective, first-line medication for alcohol use disorder. However, the 2 tablets 3 times a day dosing increases the pill burden and may be difficult for patients to take consistently. [11] The medication cannot be used in renal failure and requires dose reduction in renal insufficiency. Acamprosate’s mechanism of action is unknown but likely modulates the GABAergic system. Ideally, the medication is started after a patient is abstinent from alcohol.

retrieved_docs.title: Addiction Relapse Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: A wide variety of medications targeting diverse pathways have been studied for methamphetamine addiction. Agents studied include antidepressants, psychostimulants, topiramate, baclofen, gabapentin, antipsychotics, N-acetylcysteine, acamprosate, oxazepam, naltrexone, atomoxetine, oxytocin, and others. However, no agents have shown clear efficacy in reducing the risk of relapse. In light of many negative studies, researchers have called for the exploration of novel agents. As a result, it is likely that effective medication treatment for methamphetamine addiction will not be available soon. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Psychiatric Complications of Bariatric Surgery -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment for Alcohol Use Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol use disorder is the most common substance use reported in the postoperative period. Acamprosate, disulfiram, and naltrexone are FDA-approved to treat alcohol use disorder. [58] Topiramate has been used off-label to treat alcohol addiction with varied success. [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Disulfiram -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Disulfiram is 1 of 3 drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of alcohol dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: Disulfiram -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Disulfiram is a Food and Drug Administration-approved medication for alcohol dependence. This activity discusses disulfiram's use as an intervention for both short and long-term management of alcohol dependency in motivated individuals. Participants will explore its mechanism of action, adverse event profile, contraindications, pharmacology, monitoring protocols, and pertinent drug interactions, facilitating a comprehensive understanding among healthcare team members. This knowledge equips professionals to tailor treatment strategies to individual patient needs, fostering informed decision-making while mitigating potential adverse effects. By focusing on the pharmacological intricacies of disulfiram, healthcare providers gain the knowledge to administer personalized care, thereby aiding patients on their journey to sobriety.

retrieved_docs.content: If any history of substance abuse, the patient must be referred to an addiction medicine specialist or psychiatrist

retrieved_docs.title: Addiction Relapse Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Naltrexone is a medication used to help prevent relapses on alcohol by reducing cravings. It is available in an oral tablet and a monthly injection. Studies have shown that the use of naltrexone is associated with a reduced risk of relapse with a number-needed-to-treat (NNT) to prevent a return to any drinking of 20. [14] Acamprosate is another medication used to help prevent relapses of alcohol use. Studies have shown an NNT to prevent a return to any drinking of 12. [14] Opioids

retrieved_docs.title: Recognizing Alcohol and Drug Impairment in the Workplace in Florida -- Treatment / Management -- Florida Mandated Impaired Practitioner Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Under Chapter 456 , there are designated treatment program requirements for impaired practitioners. In Florida, a health professional can avoid disciplinary action if they complete the following: Admit the impairment Voluntarily enroll in a treatment program Withdraw or limit practice as prescribed by the impairment consultant managing the case Release all medical records to the consultant managing the case

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1512
retrieved_docs.content: Naltrexone is classified as an alcohol‑cessation agent, meaning it belongs to the group of medications used to aid individuals in withdrawing from or stopping alcohol consumption. This categorization highlights its therapeutic role in the management of alcohol dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2866
retrieved_docs.content: Clorazepate may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, indicating its potential therapeutic role in managing conditions associated with alcohol use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2385
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol Deterrents/Anti-craving agents are classified as a subcategory of naltrexone hydrochloride, and naltrexone hydrochloride is itself placed within the broader category of الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية (dependence on opioid compounds). This hierarchy indicates that naltrexone hydrochloride is a medication primarily associated with the management of opioid dependence, and that formulations used to deter alcohol consumption or reduce craving are considered specific applications of this drug. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the shared pharmacologic basis between treatments for alcohol craving and opioid dependence, supporting more integrated and informed approaches to managing substance‑use disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are classified as a subset of متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول (alcohol withdrawal syndrome). Clorazepate may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, providing therapeutic benefit in managing the withdrawal symptoms associated with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Naltrexone hydrochloride is directly linked to opioid dependence (الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية), serving as a pharmacologic agent used to treat and prevent dependence on opioid compounds. By antagonizing opioid receptors, naltrexone hydrochloride reduces cravings and blocks the effects of opioids, highlighting its role in the management of opioid use disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in alcohol dependence are classified as drugs used in addictive disorders, indicating that they belong to the broader category of medications employed to treat various addictive conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Disulfiram, a medication employed to promote abstinence in individuals with alcohol dependence, is contraindicated with agents of the paraldehyde class; therefore, co‑administration of disulfiram and paraldehyde should be avoided to prevent harmful drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with حالة ارتباكية, indicating that patients with alcohol-related conditions may experience a confusional state. Clorazepate may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, suggesting that this benzodiazepine can help manage symptoms or complications arising from the disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2392
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are conditions linked to chronic alcohol consumption, and pharmacologic management may involve benzodiazepine agents. Clorazepate is a benzodiazepine that may be employed to treat alcohol-related disorders, offering sedation and seizure prophylaxis during withdrawal. Prazepam, another benzodiazepine, is also indicated as a possible treatment option for alcohol-related disorders, providing similar therapeutic benefits. Utilization of these medications should be individualized, considering efficacy, safety, and potential for dependence, to optimize patient care in the management of alcohol-related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2693
retrieved_docs.content: Akamprosát and naltrexon are both classified as odvykací prostředky alkoholu, indicating that each belongs to the therapeutic class of agents used to facilitate alcohol withdrawal and support abstinence. As members of this medication group, they are employed in the management of alcohol dependence, providing complementary pharmacologic strategies aimed at reducing cravings and preventing relapse.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1445
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving is classified as a specific application of naltrexone hydrochloride, indicating that naltrexone hydrochloride serves as the parent compound for this anti‑craving agent. Both Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving and the condition الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية (opioid dependence) are encompassed within the broader therapeutic class Anti‑Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents. This hierarchy shows that naltrexone hydrochloride, a medication used to treat الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية, also underlies the alcohol‑deterrent formulation, linking the treatment of opioid dependence and alcohol craving under a common anti‑addiction pharmacologic framework. Managing patients with either alcohol craving or opioid dependence may therefore involve agents that belong to the Anti‑Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents category, with naltrexone hydrochloride playing a central role.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1864
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with a confusional state (حالة ارتباكية) and are also linked to hallucinations (الهَلْوَسة). Management of alcohol-related disorders may include the use of benzodiazepine agents such as clorazepate and prazepam, which are considered potential treatments for the symptoms and complications of these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1725
retrieved_docs.content: Naltrexone is classified as a member of the group “Drugs used in alcohol dependence” and is also identified as an “odvykací prostředky alkoholu” (an alcohol‑cessation agent). Consequently, naltrexone is recognized as a therapeutic medication employed in the treatment and management of alcohol dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2845
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with حالة ارتباكية, indicating that individuals with these disorders may experience a confusional state. Alcohol-related disorders also have a documented relationship with الهَلْوَسة, suggesting an additional clinical link that may be relevant for assessment. The use of beta-D-glucopyranose is contraindicated in the context of alcohol-related disorders, reflecting a potential adverse interaction with this disease condition. Pharmacologically, clorazepate may be employed to treat alcohol-related disorders, and prazepam is also listed as a possible therapeutic option. Recognizing these associations and treatment considerations is important for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with تسمم كحولي (alcohol poisoning). Both clorazepate and prazepam may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, offering benzodiazepine‑based therapeutic options. Recognizing the connection between alcohol-related disorders and acute alcohol poisoning, and the potential benefit of these medications, can help clinicians provide comprehensive care.
